sections.heading: What is BMR and Why It Matters
sections.content: Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) is the number of calories your body needs to maintain basic physiological functions at rest, such as breathing, circulation, and cell production.

subSections.heading: Weight Management
subSections.content: Knowing your BMR helps you lose, gain, or maintain weight efficiently.

subSections.heading: Fitness Planning
subSections.content: Design diet and exercise routines based on daily calorie needs.

subSections.heading: Health Monitoring
subSections.content: Understand metabolism and energy expenditure for long-term health.

subSections.heading: Foundation of TDEE
subSections.content: BMR forms the basis for Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE).

sections.heading: Understanding TDEE: Total Daily Energy Expenditure
sections.content: Your TDEE is the total number of calories you burn in a day, factoring in physical activity.

subSections.heading: Sedentary
subSections.content: Little to no exercise. Multiplier: 1.2

subSections.heading: Light Activity
subSections.content: Light exercise 1–3 days/week. Multiplier: 1.375

subSections.heading: Moderate Activity
subSections.content: Moderate exercise 3–5 days/week. Multiplier: 1.55

subSections.heading: Very Active
subSections.content: Intense exercise 6–7 days/week. Multiplier: 1.725

subSections.heading: Extra Active
subSections.content: Physically demanding job or training twice daily. Multiplier: 1.9

sections.heading: How to Use This BMR Calculator
sections.content: Follow these steps to calculate your BMR and TDEE:

subSections.heading: Step 1
subSections.content: Enter your details – Age, gender, height (cm), weight (kg), and activity level.

subSections.heading: Step 2
subSections.content: Click 'Calculate' – your BMR and TDEE will be displayed instantly.

subSections.heading: Step 3
subSections.content: Interpret results – use BMR/TDEE to plan weight loss, gain, or maintenance.

sections.heading: BMR Formulas Explained
sections.content: Different formulas exist for calculating BMR accurately:

subSections.heading: Mifflin-St Jeor Formula
subSections.content: Most accurate for adults. Men: (10 × weight) + (6.25 × height) - (5 × age) + 5. Women: (10 × weight) + (6.25 × height) - (5 × age) - 161.

subSections.heading: Harris-Benedict Formula
subSections.content: An older formula that may slightly overestimate BMR.

subSections.heading: Katch-McArdle Formula
subSections.content: Ideal for those with known lean body mass.

sections.heading: Tips to Naturally Increase Your BMR
sections.content: Boost your metabolism with these strategies:

subSections.heading: Strength Training
subSections.content: Build lean muscle mass.

subSections.heading: High-Protein Diet
subSections.content: Boost thermogenesis with protein.

subSections.heading: Stay Active
subSections.content: Increase NEAT (non-exercise activity thermogenesis).

subSections.heading: Get Enough Sleep
subSections.content: Ensure optimal metabolic function.

subSections.heading: Stay Hydrated
subSections.content: Water intake supports metabolism.

faq.q: How accurate is a BMR calculator?
faq.a: Accuracy depends on the formula used and input precision. Mifflin-St Jeor is the most reliable for adults.

faq.q: What’s the difference between BMR and TDEE?
faq.a: BMR measures calories at rest, while TDEE includes all daily activity. TDEE = BMR × activity factor.

faq.q: Can I use BMR to lose weight?
faq.a: Yes. Consume fewer calories than your TDEE to lose weight, or more to gain weight.

faq.q: How many calories do I burn a day?
faq.a: Your TDEE provides an estimate based on your BMR and activity level.

faq.q: Which BMR formula should I use?
faq.a: Mifflin-St Jeor is best for general use, while Katch-McArdle suits those who know their body fat percentage.

cta.heading: Calculate Your BMR and TDEE Today
cta.content: Use our free BMR Calculator to understand your metabolism, daily calorie needs, and take control of your health and fitness goals.
